Winning Pipeline

2. Variable Importance

💡 Interpreting Variable Importance
Higher bar = more influential on the model's predictions.
Built-in importance comes from the model itself (e.g., how often a feature is used in tree splits). Permutation importance measures how much the score degrades when a feature's values are randomly shuffled.
Caveat: Correlated features can split importance between them, making each look less important than it truly is.
Business tip: Focus data-quality and feature-engineering efforts on the top features. Features with near-zero importance may be candidates for removal, simplifying the model without hurting performance.

Method: built-in

2b. SHAP Analysis

💡 Understanding SHAP Values

SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) provides a principled way to explain individual predictions by assigning each feature a contribution value based on cooperative game theory.

  • Waterfall plot: Decomposes a single prediction, showing how each feature shifts the output from the baseline (average prediction) to the final value. This answers "why did the model make this specific prediction?"
  • Beeswarm plot: Shows the distribution of SHAP values across all samples. Features at the top have the largest overall impact. The color-value relationship reveals direction — e.g., if high values of RECENT_3M_EVENTS (red dots) appear on the right (positive SHAP), that feature increases predicted propensity.
  • Heatmap: Reveals patterns across samples. Clusters of similar SHAP patterns indicate distinct subgroups in your data that the model treats differently.

Key difference from Variable Importance: Feature importance (Section 2) shows how much a feature matters overall. SHAP shows how and in which direction each feature drives predictions, including non-linear and interaction effects.

SHAP Waterfall (Single Prediction)

Shows how each feature pushes the prediction from the base value (average model output) to the final prediction for a representative sample. Red bars push the prediction higher, blue bars push it lower.

SHAP Beeswarm (Global Impact)

Each dot is one sample. Position on x-axis shows the SHAP value (impact on prediction). Color indicates the feature value (red = high, blue = low). Features are sorted by overall importance. This reveals both the magnitude and direction of each feature's effect.

SHAP Heatmap (Sample × Feature)

Shows SHAP values across all samples (rows) and features (columns). Samples are ordered by similarity, revealing clusters of predictions driven by similar feature patterns. Red cells indicate positive SHAP contributions, blue cells indicate negative.

3. Feature Interactions

💡 Understanding Feature Interactions
An interaction means two features together predict better than either alone.
The interaction strength number shows how much extra predictive power the pair provides beyond their individual contributions.
How to use this: Strong interactions suggest the relationship between these features and the target is non-linear or conditional — e.g., “feature A only matters when feature B is high.”
Note on SHAP: For deeper interaction analysis, SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values can decompose individual predictions. SHAP interaction values show exactly how pairs of features jointly influence each prediction, going beyond the aggregate view shown here.
#Feature AFeature BPair auc A aloneB aloneInteraction

+0.0437

4a. Best Pipeline Evolution

💡 Reading the Evolution Diagram
This table uses a genetic algorithm metaphor: pipelines are “organisms” and their building blocks (imputer, scaler, feature selector, algorithm) are “genes.”
Block switching is like biological crossover — successful components from different parent pipelines are recombined to create better offspring.
Each row is a milestone where the best pipeline improved. Coloured tags on the right show which type of genetic operation caused the improvement (algorithm swap, block mutation, or parameter tweak).

4b. Score Progression

💡 Interpreting the Score Curve
Steep early rise = easy initial gains from finding a good algorithm family.
Flat plateau = diminishing returns; the search has likely found a near-optimal region.
If the curve plateaus early, the problem may be relatively simple or the budget generous. A late plateau suggests the search benefited from extended exploration.
The gap between start and end represents the total improvement found by evolution.

4c. Algorithm Diversity Over Time

💡 What Algorithm Diversity Shows
Algorithm diversity measures how many different ML algorithm families are represented in the population at each generation.
Early generations typically explore many algorithm families. As evolution progresses, the best-performing type dominates. A rapid convergence to one algorithm (like XGBoost) suggests it is clearly superior for this problem.
If diversity stays high throughout, the problem may be ambiguous or the search space may need refinement.

4d. Genetic Operator Impact

💡 Genetic Operator Types Explained
Algorithm swap: Completely replacing the ML algorithm (e.g., RandomForest → XGBoost). Highest impact, rarest.
Block crossover / mutation: Swapping or adding preprocessing / feature-selection steps. Moderate impact.
Hyperparameter tweak: Fine-tuning numbers within the same algorithm (e.g., learning_rate, n_estimators). Smallest per-step impact but most frequent.
Typical pattern: Algorithm swaps make the biggest early jumps, then block mutations and parameter tweaks refine. Diminishing improvements are normal and expected in evolutionary optimisation.

Operator Contribution to Improvements

Total improvements: 18

Hyperparameter tweak
3 (17%)
Algorithm swap
2 (11%)

2b. SHAP Analysis

💡 Understanding SHAP Values

SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) provides a principled way to explain individual predictions by assigning each feature a contribution value based on cooperative game theory.

  • Waterfall plot: Decomposes a single prediction, showing how each feature shifts the output from the baseline (average prediction) to the final value. This answers "why did the model make this specific prediction?"
  • Beeswarm plot: Shows the distribution of SHAP values across all samples. Features at the top have the largest overall impact. The color-value relationship reveals direction — e.g., if high values of RECENT_3M_EVENTS (red dots) appear on the right (positive SHAP), that feature increases predicted propensity.

Key difference from Variable Importance: Feature importance (Section 2) shows how much a feature matters overall. SHAP shows how and in which direction each feature drives predictions, including non-linear and interaction effects.
